documents.title: anthropic-stem-fellowship
documents.outcome:
**Substantial close-read pass on Q1 + Q2 + Q3 ¶1 landed (May 14); drafts now plain-text and paste-ready for Greenhouse.** Q1 rewritten across all three paragraphs — umbrella opener (weak gravitational lensing), chronological PhD→postdoc arc with "difficult reality of cosmological data" framing and 3–6-year analysis foreshadow, CEA Paris-Saclay credentials cluster with CNRS glossed at first mention. Q2 restructured into 2 paragraphs + handoff — ML knowledge + Bayesian/frequentist substrate + bias-vs-variance argument; word2vec/Foucault final project with ultimate-point sentence + TTS/STT + Pleias reframed for synthetic-data scaling. Q3 ¶1 added orienting sentence, replaced "results" with "null-test validation," added arXiv link inline, rewrote role-shift around "constructing back pressure that allows the agent to check its own work." CV: in-prep language replaced with "submitted to A&A"; publication counts synced at 4 further refereed. All markdown stripped from Q1–Q5 prose. **References (Lanusse + Holder) confirmed — Cail has been in touch.**

**Remaining for Cail:** close-read on Q3 ¶2–4 (Lightcone substrate, day-to-day workflow, multi-language tools), Q4 (three project ideas + Anthropic motivation + France–Anthropic closer), Q5 (reference context paragraphs). CV interactive walk-through still parked at [[application/anthropic-stem-fellowship/cv-rebuild]] (header shape, Lightcone wording trim, CNRS phrasing, any 2026 additions, Skills "AI & workflows" line). Optional INSPIRE eyeball. **Paste into Greenhouse and submit by Friday May 15.**
documents.body: Anthropic STEM Fellowship Apply to the Anthropic STEM Fellowship . High-reward, deadline May 15, 2026 . Base on the CNRS AI Rising Talents application ( ai-futures/cnrs-5pp/ ), but the shape is fundamentally different — short-essay form, not a dossier. See anthropic-call-requirements for the full call: 5 textareas + CV + 2 references, no word limits. Audience flip from CNRS CNRS: AI/CS committee evaluating a cosmologist’s research programme. Lead with cosmology, motivate it as the proving ground for agentic science, position AI as the practice that makes cosmology better. Anthropic: AI researchers evaluating a domain expert who already lives inside Claude Code. They know agents deeply; they don’t know weak lensing. Lead with the cosmologist who is already deep in real, daily, frontier Claude-Code-mediated science — domain expertise + lived agentic practice as the offering. Strengths to lean on Lived Claude Code practice at unusual depth. UNIONS B-mode workflow (~10k lines agent-written, 120k total), felt, shuttle, lightcone — all real, all in production, all defensible. Question 3 (Claude Code usage) is where most applicants will be thin and Cail can be deep. PhD in a hard STEM field with first-author frontier results. SPT-3G CMB lensing, UNIONS B-mode lead author, SPT × Euclid MoU first author, h-index 19, ~1,640 citations. A view on what “doing science with AI” actually means — agentic science framing, tapestry as verification infrastructure, the bitter-lesson scaffolding with the verification counter-theme. Cail has thought about this carefully and at length. Working architecture per question Sketches per question — entry shape, draw-from, length target. To shape with Cail; nothing here is committed until he reads it. Q1 — STEM Expertise “Please share the STEM field(s) where you have the most expertise and describe your relevant experience/contribution.” Lift from: cnrs-5pp/sections/research-report.tex opener (the LCDM-and-systematics paragraphs that survived the close-read pass), compressed to ~3 short paragraphs. Frame: observational cosmology, specifically the percent-precision systematics regime where the field now lives. Spine: (1) what cosmology is converging toward and where the open questions are, (2) Cail’s specific corner — CMB lensing with SPT-3G, cosmic shear with UNIONS, Euclid×CMB cross-correlations, kinematic lensing supervision; the throughline is characterizing systematics , (3) concrete output — lead-author UNIONS B -mode paper, SPT-3G lensing pipeline, SPT × Euclid MoU first author, bibliometrics line. Voice: arrive through content; first sentence is about the universe, not about Cail. Q2 — ML/AI Experience (locked: honest + texture) “Describe any experience you have with ML/AI, e.g. training custom models, applying an existing model, using adjacent techniques, etc.” Three short paragraphs. Honest opener. Cail is a domain scientist, not an ML researcher. Name that cleanly. The small ML usage that is real and distinctively his. word2vec for a Foucault philosophy project (2018) + text-to-speech models run locally. Dates the timeline as someone curious about these tools years before the LLM party; texture, not padding. The statistical substrate of his actual work. Bayesian inference, MCMC, hierarchical modeling, likelihood design, posterior validation (AU Mic, UNIONS, SPT-3G). Then the soft on-ramp: the more substantive recent AI experience has been applying frontier LLMs as collaborators in scientific workflows — see Q3. Brevity is the signal. Most applicants will pad Q2 by reaching; Cail being short and pointing forward distinguishes him. Q3 — Claude Code Usage (the showpiece) “Describe any experience you have with Claude Code or similar agentic coding products. If you have used them before, please describe your use case(s) and workflow.” Where Cail wins or loses this application. The shape, with Lightcone now forward: ¶1 — The artifact. UNIONS B -mode paper (Daley et al. 2026), conducted almost entirely through Claude Code: ~10k lines of agent-written analysis code, figures, manuscript; ~120k lines edited across the workflow. Peer-reviewed; presented at Moriond; the role-shift (architect rather than implementer); since mid-2025 Cail has worked this way exclusively. ¶2 — The substrate Cail co-builds. Lightcone Research as joint CNRS–Berkeley open-source project building the open ecosystem for reproducible, composable, and verifiable scientific research in the age of agentic AI (their own public framing). Cail’s contributions: felt (Go CLI he maintains, tracks decisions/findings as structured fibers), ASTRA (open YAML schema for analysis nodes carrying decisions + alternatives + evidence anchors), Vellum (viewer that renders an analysis as browsable provenance graph). Anchor: “Lightcone is the infrastructure AI co-scientists are missing” — public framing, complement-not-competitor to Claude Code. Lets us wave at the value-prop in their language. ¶3 — The day-to-day workflow. Fiber → Claude Code (surveys, drafts against constitution, reports at handoffs) → shuttle (cross-session handoff to fresh agent against same constitution) → verification. Posture: human as architect, agent as implementer ; structured record makes review tractable at this volume. Reflexive close: “This application is being co-drafted with Claude” — embodies Anthropic’s own transparency principle. Charming if it lands, easily cuttable if it doesn’t. Scientist-not-builder anchoring throughout. Cail contributes to and maintains — not “co-founded” or “built.” The project is the open ecosystem I work inside , not the startup I’m in . Larry Page funding mentioned in voice; left out of the written draft (private, and CNRS+Berkeley is sufficient public framing). Researcher verbs. Drawing-from: the agentic-science section of CNRS 5pp, reshaped — flip from “selling agentic science to AI evaluators” to “telling AI evaluators how I already do this.” Reader knows agents; speak peer-to-peer. Longest of the five. Still well under a dossier section. Q4 — Project Ideas “Propose one or two concrete ideas for evaluating, improving, or applying Claude in your field that you think are promising and would be excited about.” Non-binding. Anthropic frames this as a taste-and-reach signal: mentors co-design the actual project. So the bar is not “this is what we should do” but “look at how I think about leveraged uses of Claude in real science.” Lead with one ambitious capability-add and one evaluation/benchmark , since the question invites both axes (“evaluating, improving, or applying”). Candidates, ranked by my read of fit (Cail to reshape): Real-cosmology benchmarks for agentic capability. Package real cosmological analyses (UNIONS B -mode validation, CMB lensing pipelines, kinematic lensing) as evaluation harnesses for Claude, scored by numerical agreement + expert rubric + LLM-as-judge. The existing AI-science benchmarks evaluate isolated tasks; this would evaluate the full workflow — data → analysis → judging the result’s trustworthiness. Initial release possible inside 3 months given Cail’s existing analyses. Aligned with Anthropic’s own move toward evaluation grounded in messy real problems (OpenAI’s Proof Q&A is the closest precedent on the model-lab side). Strength: Cail is uniquely positioned; Anthropic doesn’t have this; deliverable inside the fellowship; useful to Anthropic past the fellowship. Provenance/verification as a first-class capability inside long-running Claude sessions. When a Claude Code session produces a multi-step scientific analysis, automatically emit an inspectable provenance graph (ASTRA-shaped: nodes for decisions, jobs, findings; edges for data flow; evidence anchors) that humans or downstream Claude instances can audit. This is a capability question for Claude itself : does the model produce reasoning that another instance can trustworthily verify? Strength: uses the tapestry/felt work Cail has already built; the science-verification thread maps onto a broader Claude capability concern (auditable agentic work). Risk: more infrastructure-shaped; needs framing that lands with Anthropic as model-capability research, not as tooling. Claude as scientific verifier (red-team mode). Use Claude to systematically critique published scientific claims — trace chains of reasoning, identify load-bearing assumptions, flag missing checks. Evaluate against historical cases where errors were eventually caught (BICEP2 dust, OPERA neutrinos) and current open tensions. Connects to the PleIAs scientific-claim-verification thread. Risk: less “build something inside Claude,” more “use Claude to verify”; possibly too applied for Anthropic’s interest. My recommendation: lead with #1 (benchmarks) , follow with #2 (provenance-as-capability) . Together they say I will build evaluation that grounds your models in real science, and I will push on the model-side capability that makes scientific Claude trustworthy. Both inside three months. Both leveraged by Cail’s existing work. To check with Cail before drafting. Q5 — References (locked: Lanusse + Holder) “Provide two references that can speak to your past work and experience.” François Lanusse — CNRS, CosmoStat, Lightcone Research co-lead. Speaks to agentic-science practice from inside; daily proximity to Cail’s Claude Code work. Gil Holder — Brand & Monica Fortner Endowed Chair in Theoretical Astrophysics, UIUC. Cail’s PhD advisor; 5 yrs close supervision on SPT-3G CMB lensing. Certifies STEM foundations. Email + contact details available in cnrs-5pp/sections/references-contacts.tex . CV Only attachment. Carries weight. The CNRS CV at cnrs-5pp/sections/cv.tex is well-structured and Anthropic-friendly already — Agentic science is the first Research subsection, supervision and outreach are present, bibliometrics are there. Two options: Reuse the CNRS CV as a standalone PDF, lightly retargeted (remove dossier-specific framing if any; tighten Research-Interests bullets toward AI/agentic-coding). Fast, defensible, already polished. Build a leaner standalone CV that pushes agentic-coding practice up and to the front. More effort; possibly redundant with what Q3 already says. My lean: option 1. The CV’s job is to certify the STEM record; Q3 carries the Claude Code story. Voice Inherits ai-futures/CLAUDE.md : Researcher verbs ( conduct, investigate, build, lead ), not builder verbs ( architect, design, create ). Arrive through content — never “In this essay I will…” ; open on the universe / the practice / the claim. No declarative slogans, no triplet punchlines, no empty signposting. Textarea register: tighter than dossier prose, but still flowing — these are short essays, not bullet lists. Bullets sparingly, where they earn their keep (e.g., distinguishing the parts of the agentic workflow in Q3). Context — Lightcone is forward Per Cail (May 12 chat): Lightcone is much bigger than the prior architecture had it. Joint CNRS–Berkeley open-source project funded by Larry Page (private; don’t write); going public this week; premier talk at Uros Seljak’s festival; six-person team with Cail fourth on the linear list; three advisors; developer summit at the end of June (during the fellowship window). Liam (Berkeley PhD with Seljak, possible Dario Amodei adjacency) is on the team. The North Star document explicitly positions Lightcone as “the infrastructure [AI co-scientists] are missing” — public framing that lands hot with Anthropic and is not me reaching for it. Implication for the application: Lightcone is forward in Q3 (substrate Cail contributes to) and provides backdrop for Q4 (project ideas land inside an ecosystem Anthropic can see, not in a vacuum). CV gets a clean Lightcone line in the agentic-science section. Scientist-not-builder anchoring throughout — Cail’s own load-bearing identity claim (“I want to remain a scientist, not a builder or businessman”). Lightcone is the ecosystem I contribute to as a scientist , never the startup I co-founded . Researcher verbs ( contribute, maintain, develop ) never builder verbs. Awareness: Liam may meet with Dario Amodei. Whatever Anthropic sees from us should not be discordant with what arrives in Dario’s awareness through Liam’s channel. Possibly worth a check-in with Liam on framing before submission. Open decisions remaining for Cail Confirmed: in-person SF + US work auth + June 15 – Sept 15. (Cail to confirm logistics and citizenship status.) Project ideas ranking and shape (Q4). Concur with benchmarks + provenance-as-capability (now Lightcone-anchored), or different lean? CV (attachment). Reuse CNRS CV as standalone, or build leaner version with Lightcone forward? Drafting cadence. Four days. Suggest: today — Q3 draft (showpiece) + Q1 draft; tomorrow — Q2 + Q4; Thursday — close-read + cohesion + CV export; Friday — submit early morning. Decisions locked (May 12) Q2 shape: honest + texture (word2vec/Foucault, Bayesian substrate, soft on-ramp to Q3). Q5 references: Lanusse + Holder. Vision placement: voice only, implicit. The beach-with-friends future animates the writing from behind; not explicitly stated. Original dispatch (preserved) I would like to apply to the Anthropic STEM Fellowship, https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/5189848008 We can base the application strongly on my cnrs ai rising talents application, although i want to put a lot of effort into it this week since it’s a hugely high-reward application. i think first step is to open a new sub-fiber with the full application requirements, including all the questions on the application with word limits, etc. then you can stay open and not kill ppid or close, and i’ll come chat and we’ll start fleshing out a plan and application

documents.title: Anthropic STEM Fellowship — call requirements
documents.outcome: Single source of truth for the application — 5 short-essay questions + CV + 2 references, no word limits, due May 15. Tight, focused, very different shape from CNRS.
documents.body: Posting: https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/5189848008 Candidate AI guidance: https://anthropic.com/candidate-ai-guidance Program shape Duration: June 15 – September 15, 2026 (multi-month; possible extension) Location: Anthropic SF office, in person , full-time Stipend: ~$3,800/week + benefits Work auth: US employment authorization required for program duration Selection stages: application → technical assessment → take-home → mentor discussion on research and project directions Application deadline: May 15, 2026 (today is May 11 — 4 days) Decision date: by June 1 Eligibility PhD in a STEM field, or equivalent research experience Familiarity with Claude and Claude Code “in regular workflow” (named explicitly as a requirement) Effectiveness in fast-paced, high-agency environments Capacity to deliver concrete results within the fellowship timeframe Enthusiasm about AI accelerating scientific discovery Note: “ML background is advantageous but not required; scientific judgment and learning agility are prioritized.” Anthropic explicitly invites applications from candidates who don’t meet every line. Application — what you actually submit Everything is inline in the Greenhouse form except the CV. No word or character limits are stated on any of the questions; the fields are textareas. No separate research-statement upload. Identity / contact: First name, Last name, Email, Phone (opt), Country. Attachment: Resume/CV — PDF, DOC, DOCX, TXT, RTF. Direct upload, Dropbox, or Google Drive. Five form questions, verbatim: STEM Expertise. “Please share the STEM field(s) where you have the most expertise and describe your relevant experience/contribution.” ML/AI Experience. “Describe any experience you have with ML/AI, e.g. training custom models, applying an existing model, using adjacent techniques, etc.” Claude Code Usage. “Describe any experience you have with Claude Code or similar agentic coding products. If you have used them before, please describe your use case(s) and workflow.” Project Ideas. “Propose one or two concrete ideas for evaluating, improving, or applying Claude in your field that you think are promising and would be excited about. (This is not necessarily what you will work on – the mentors will help co-design the actual project – but it helps us understand your interests.)” References. “Provide two references that can speak to your past work and experience. Please include their name, email address (or preferred contact method), and any additional context about your collaboration.” How this differs from CNRS The CNRS application is a ~25-page dossier pitched to a French AI/CS committee, with a 5pp research programme as the centerpiece and a counter-theme of thoroughness over speed. This one is the opposite shape: Five textareas + CV. No long-form research statement, no programme, no 5pp. Audience knows AI deeply, not cosmology. The framing flips: CNRS sold cosmology-with-AI to AI people; this sells the cosmologist who is already deep in real Claude-Code-mediated frontier science to Anthropic. In-person SF. Three months on-site. Not a research programme — a fellowship engagement collaborating with research teams. “Concrete project ideas,” explicitly non-binding. Mentors co-design. So the project-ideas answer is a signal of taste and reach , not a commitment. The CNRS dossier is a strong source for: bibliometrics, the UNIONS B-mode story, the agentic-science framing, the verification/tapestry threads, the SPT × Euclid MoU language, the publications narrative. Lift what fits; reshape voice for the new audience. Anthropic’s AI-usage guidance for applicants Three principles, paraphrased then quoted where exact: Use Claude thoughtfully. AI as collaborator to strengthen applications. “Be yourself.” Refine, don’t replace. Authenticity over polish. “Be transparent.” Same honesty Anthropic applies to its own processes. Allowed: polishing your own drafts, refining articulation, researching Anthropic, practicing interviews. Not allowed: AI on coding assessments (unless explicitly permitted), fabricating experience, live-interview assistance, AI substituting for original thinking. Implication for this application: Claude can polish what Cail has actually done. It cannot manufacture project ideas Cail hasn’t reasoned about, or invent Claude Code workflows Cail hasn’t lived. The Claude Code Usage question in particular wants lived practice — and Cail has unusually deep, well-documented lived practice (felt, shuttle, lightcone, the UNIONS workflow, this very conversation).

documents.title: CV rebuild — Anthropic STEM Fellowship
documents.outcome:
Walkthrough pass with Cail landed. Standalone CV at `cv-rebuild/cv.tex` is in submission-ready shape (3 pages, compiles cleanly; `cv.pdf` is gitignored per the loom policy added this session — rebuild with `pdflatex cv.tex`).

**Settled this session:**
- **Title block**: "Cail Daley" in **EB Garamond** bold smallcaps (not Raleway), subheading *"Postdoctoral Researcher · Cosmology and Agentic Science"* below in smaller smallcaps, thin rule. Title-to-subtitle and subtitle-to-rule spacing balanced. Running header band dropped from all pages; centered page-number footers only.
- **Research Interests grid**: AI items lead (*Agentic science methodology* | *Verification of AI-mediated research*) in row 1; cosmology fills rows 2–3 (*Cosmic shear* | *CMB lensing & cross-correlations*; *Lensing systematics* | *Kinematic lensing*).
- **Citation line**: settled at **"h-index 19; ~1,800 citations to refereed publications"** — rounded up from the precise 1,769 in the refreshed snapshot [[bibliometrics-may-2026]]. INSPIRE-HEP and ORCID hyperlinked.
- **Lightcone bullet** (under *Agentic science — 2025–present*): *"Core team member of [Lightcone Research](https://lightconeresearch.org), a joint CNRS–Berkeley open-source project developing the [ASTRA](https://astra-spec.org) schema and surrounding infrastructure for reproducible, composable, and verifiable AI-mediated science. Maintain [felt](https://github.com/cailmdaley/felt), a Go CLI for tracking analysis decisions and findings as structured fibers."* Three teal hyperlinks; ASTRA correctly framed as Lightcone's deliverable, not a parallel project.
- **Workshop entry** at top of *Outreach & Service*: *"09/2026 — DAp SciAI Days, CEA Paris-Saclay (forthcoming): co-organize the department's three-day workshop on AI for scientific research; lead Day 2 on agentic research, including a live Claude Code demo and panel discussion."*
- **Loom gitignore policy** (separate commit `311712ff`): aggressive-ignore for `*.pdf`, `*.png`, `*.jpg`, `*.jpeg` + LaTeX scratch. `cv.pdf` removed from tracking. Existing PNGs across the loom were *not* mass-untracked — Cail flagged that decision as a follow-up audit.

**Open follow-ups (none blocking submission):**
1. **Q3 consistency in `ai-futures/anthropic-stem/drafts.md`**: currently says *"core contributor to Lightcone Research"*; should swap to *"core team member"* to match the CV's framing.
2. **Skills "AI & workflows" row**: surfaced as candidate for harder Claude Code emphasis; not changed this pass (researcher-not-builder anchor weighed against pitch-deck adjacency).
3. **Daley+26 publication entry**: surfaced as candidate (currently absorbed into the bibliometrics summary line); not broken out.
4. **Portolan public Friday**: if it ships before submission, a fourth hyperlink could land — explicitly scoped out of this pass to avoid a half-broken link risk.
5. **Existing tracked PNGs in the loom** (~30 across portolan/dossier/analysis-frontispiece): policy now says default-ignore, but existing tracked files weren't bulk-removed. Separate audit pass when Cail wants.
documents.body: CV rebuild — Anthropic STEM Fellowship Context Cail is applying to the Anthropic STEM Fellowship — deadline May 15 2026. The CV is the only attachment to the Greenhouse form and carries weight independently of the textarea answers. The parent application fiber is application/anthropic-stem-fellowship . Read its constitution first — audience, voice principles, the Q3 / Q4 architecture this CV needs to be consistent with. The form-question drafts live at ai-futures/anthropic-stem/drafts.md . Mode Interactive. Cail will attach and shape the CV with you collaboratively. Do not charge ahead autonomously — read the context, then work with him. Aesthetic and formatting calls in particular are taste-y and his to make; expect to surface candidates and proposals rather than committing on his behalf. Source Current CV (LaTeX, currently included by the CNRS dossier): ai-futures/cnrs-5pp/sections/cv.tex Built dossier PDF (CV pages within): ai-futures/cnrs-5pp/Daley_CNRS_AI_Rising_Talents_2026.pdf Dossier compile context: ai-futures/cnrs-5pp/CLAUDE.md Two confirmed additions Lightcone Research line under the Agentic science (2025–present) Research subsection. See the parent fiber’s Q3 framing for how Lightcone is positioned in this application; the CV line should match. CNRS AI Rising Talents interview line under Awards & Fellowships — Selected for interview, 2026. Confirm exact phrasing with Cail. Plus the aesthetic / formatting cleanup Cail wants to walk through together, plus any 2026 additions (talks, supervision, papers) he surfaces in session. Standing preferences Researcher verbs throughout ( conduct, lead, build, characterize, supervise, contribute, maintain, develop ). No builder-verb creep. Do not report page count after edits unless Cail explicitly asks. Keep the CV consistent with the form-question drafts at ai-futures/anthropic-stem/drafts.md — any position, paper, talk, or role should match what’s claimed there. On exit Update outcome: to reflect what was settled and where the standalone PDF lives. Anything Cail flagged for later — typography choices not yet made, additions he wasn’t sure about — should surface there so they don’t get lost.

documents.title: Application drafts
documents.outcome: Five textareas + Q5 references; close-reading in progress.
documents.body: Anthropic STEM Fellowship — Application Drafts Deadline: May 15, 2026 · Submission: Greenhouse form · Constitution & meta: application/anthropic-stem-fellowship Five textareas + CV (only attachment) + two references. No word limits stated; textarea convention reads as “short and tight.” Q1 — STEM Expertise Please share the STEM field(s) where you have the most expertise and describe your relevant experience/contribution. Observational cosmology studies the contents and history of the universe through the light that reaches us across cosmic time. Over the past century, cosmologists have converged on a consensus model that fits a wide range of observables including the cosmic microwave background, the large-scale distribution of galaxies, supernova distances, and light-element abundances with just six parameters. This model is named ΛCDM after its dominant components, dark energy (Λ) and cold dark matter (CDM), which together make up 95% of the universe yet are detectable only through their impact on spacetime. As a result, ΛCDM can only describe the dominant components of the universe phenomenologically. Modern cosmology largely involves pushing measurements to higher precision across complementary probes and asking whether they reveal tensions in ΛCDM that might point to a more fundamental model. With the next generation of cosmology experiments coming online, including Euclid, LSST, and Simons Observatory, statistical errors will shrink by another order of magnitude in the coming years, and systematic uncertainties will dominate every measurement. My research has focused on mapping the distribution of matter in the universe with weak gravitational lensing. During my PhD I worked on CMB lensing with the South Pole Telescope’s third-generation receiver (SPT-3G), building one of three independent pipelines for SPT-3G’s first flagship lensing analysis. In my postdoctoral work I have moved to weak lensing of galaxy shapes with the UNIONS optical survey, cross-correlations of SPT-3G CMB lensing with Euclid, and the emerging field of kinematic lensing which uses galaxy rotation rather than shape. My work across all of these has centered on the difficult reality of cosmological data — building pipelines for processing and analysis, characterizing systematics (often astrophysics in their own right), and cross-validating results across independent methods. An observational cosmology analysis typically takes three to six years from initial data to final cosmological results, and much of that time is spent characterizing the impact of analysis choices and systematics on the data. I am a postdoctoral researcher at CEA Paris-Saclay’s Département d’Astrophysique (UMR AIM / CosmoStat), where I led the principal null-test validation for the first UNIONS weak lensing cosmology release and now lead a Memorandum of Understanding between the SPT and Euclid collaborations for CMB-lensing × galaxy-shear cross-correlations targeting Euclid’s first data release in October 2026. I am first author on two publications, have made substantive individual contributions to four further refereed papers, and am co-author on more than thirty (h-index 19, ~1,800 citations on INSPIRE-HEP). The CNRS (France’s national research agency) has invited me to interview in mid-June as a finalist for the two AI Rising Talents fellowship positions awarded this year, for which I have proposed a four-year research program in observational cosmology structured around developing the practice of agentic AI for science. Q2 — ML/AI Experience Describe any experience you have with ML/AI, e.g. training custom models, applying an existing model, using adjacent techniques, etc. I have worked alongside machine learning cosmologists throughout my career and have a general understanding of basic ML methods and the architectures of neural networks and large language models. I have not trained or applied custom models in my own research, and I would not represent myself as part of that work. My work relies on more standard Bayesian and frequentist statistical techniques, including MCMC samplers, two-point statistics, and semi-analytic covariances. In cosmology, characterizing systematic errors tends to be more important than minimizing statistical uncertainties, and methods that can be decomposed and cross-checked at every step tend to be more reliable than neural-network-based estimators. My other engagements with ML lie outside my cosmology research. As an undergraduate in 2018 I trained a word2vec model on a corpus of nineteenth-century texts on the prison and the penal system as the final project for a class on Foucault, treating word co-occurrence as a computational analog of his “invisible ensembles” of statements. I argued that the distributional geometry such models learn could serve as a computational instrument for Foucauldian discourse analysis, making visible the relations between statements that traditional reading misses. More recently, I run text-to-speech and speech-to-text models locally for research and personal use. I have also begun a collaboration with Pleias, the French open-model laboratory whose synthetic data work has become central to recent frontier LLM scaling. The shared project is a three-stage scientific-claim verification pipeline — claim extraction, evidence retrieval, and per-claim labeling — comparing domain-fine-tuned smaller models against general-purpose frontier LLMs on real scientific verification tasks; cosmology is the proposed lead domain. At the center of my recent AI work, though, is the daily collaboration with frontier large language models in cosmological research itself — which I describe in the next question. Q3 — Claude Code Usage Describe any experience you have with Claude Code or similar agentic coding products. If you have used them before, please describe your use case(s) and workflow. I started using Claude Code in summer 2025. Since then it has become the singular interface between me and all aspects of my work, including analysis code, manuscript preparation, literature search, note-taking, and meeting transcripts. The first UNIONS weak-lensing cosmology null-test validation (Daley et al. 2026, https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03227) was produced with about 10,000 lines of agent-written analysis code, figures, and manuscript text, against roughly 120,000 lines edited across the full workflow. As I have become more competent in agentic research, my usage has shifted from prompting script writing and debugging to higher levels of abstraction: analysis design, specifying test criteria, and — most importantly — constructing back pressure that allows the agent to check its own work. Working this way made it practical to explore a broader space of methodological choices, run validation checks that would otherwise have been triaged for lack of time, and document every decision in a structured record. Alongside the practice, I help build the substrate that makes it rigorous. I am a core team member of Lightcone Research, a joint open-source project of the CNRS and UC Berkeley developing the open ecosystem for reproducible, composable, and verifiable scientific research in the age of agentic AI. The premise is that scientific knowledge does not accumulate in papers but in the decisions behind them. The central artifact is ASTRA , an open YAML schema in which an analysis carries its decisions, alternatives considered, and evidence anchors alongside its data and code. I contribute to its development and maintain felt , a Go CLI for tracking decisions and findings as a structured graph of markdown fibers. Lightcone positions itself as the infrastructure AI co-scientists are missing — a complement, not a competitor, to the kind of work Claude Code already does well. The day-to-day shape: I open a fiber for whatever decision the analysis needs to make; Claude Code surveys related code and prior fibers, drafts against a constitution I have written, and reports back at chosen handoff points. Multi-session work hands off through shuttle , an orchestration tool I also maintain — in spirit, my version of what OpenAI’s Symphony does for agent dispatch, though grown out of the practice rather than from a system design. The verification posture throughout is “human as architect, agent as implementer”: I specify what should be true and design the tests for it; the agent does the work; I inspect what was decided and why, and the structured record is what makes that inspection tractable at this volume of code. I have built tools for agent orchestration and context management across Go, Elixir, and Rust — none of which I write directly. They have emerged less as products than as ways to learn the principles from the inside. felt is now used by a handful of collaborators for their own scientific work. Q4 — Project Ideas Propose one or two concrete ideas for evaluating, improving, or applying Claude in your field that you think are promising and would be excited about. (This is not necessarily what you will work on – the mentors will help co-design the actual project – but it helps us understand your interests.) The closest existing AI-for-science benchmarks evaluate isolated capabilities or extract tasks from published papers. What I would most want to build is something different: full evaluation environments drawn from real cosmological analyses, complete with the standard mess that distinguishes a working dataset from a sanitized one. Real survey data — UNIONS galaxy shape catalogs, CMB power spectra, kinematic lensing pilot data — together with the systematics that have not been fully characterized, the mask boundaries, the calibration jitter, the format quirks that anyone who has worked with cosmological data will recognize. The agent’s task is to deliver a cosmological result that survives joint scrutiny: expert rubric, numerical agreement against a withheld reference, and LLM-as-judge scoring. This is the part of doing science most current benchmarks step around, and it is also the part where domain expertise matters most. An initial set, drawn from the UNIONS and SPT analyses I have already led, would be deliverable within the fellowship, and the format generalizes to other observational fields. I am also interested in the inverse mode within the same project: substantial synthetic environments designed around problems that require taste rather than rote correctness — scenarios with multiple defensible answers where the cosmologist’s judgment about which assumption to commit to matters more than the numerical answer. Synthetic environments have the advantage of being clean enough to evaluate definitively, and the open question is whether they can be designed to capture the things that make real cosmology genuinely hard, or whether the loss of mess strips out exactly what one wants to evaluate. The question of how much real-data difficulty can be carried into synthetic generation is itself worth investigating, and the two modes — real and synthetic — would inform each other. A complementary, live-rather-than-evaluative project would take a serious open cosmology problem and see how far a domain expert paired with Claude can push it inside three months. Candidates within my reach include accelerated systematics exploration in the UNIONS multi-probe pipeline, characterization of post-Born curl-mode CMB lensing in cross-correlation with galaxy surveys, or methodological work on kinematic lensing as an emerging probe. The point would be less to produce a publishable cosmology result inside the fellowship — some of these have natural longer timescales — than to learn what concentrating the model against a real cosmological problem with someone who knows the domain in detail actually unlocks, and where it is presently weakest. Two further interests inform why Anthropic in particular draws me to this work: alignment and model behavior beyond what my technical work has so far directly engaged with, and the phenomenology of working closely with frontier models in collaborative settings — what the experience is actually like, for researcher and model both, when the work is shared. Underneath all of this is a broader motivation. The French research community where I am based has deep capability in observational cosmology and statistical methods, and limited prior working contact with frontier AI laboratories. Beyond whatever the fellowship produces directly, I would like to help open the working relationship between European scientific research and Anthropic, and to leave it stronger than I found it. Q5 — References Provide two references that can speak to your past work and experience. Please include their name, email address (or preferred contact method), and any additional context about your collaboration. François Lanusse Chargé de Recherche CNRS · CosmoStat, CEA Paris-Saclay francois.lanusse@cea.fr Colleague at CosmoStat since I joined as a postdoc in fall 2024. We collaborate closely on Lightcone Research, the joint CNRS–Berkeley project building the open ecosystem for reproducible, composable, and verifiable AI-mediated science; I am a core team member and François co-leads the project. He has daily proximity to my agentic research practice and can speak to both the scientific quality of the work and the way I work with frontier models on real cosmological problems. Gil Holder Brand & Monica Fortner Endowed Chair in Theoretical Astrophysics · University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign gholder@illinois.edu My Ph.D. advisor (2019–2024). Gil supervised my doctoral research on CMB lensing measurements with the South Pole Telescope’s third-generation receiver, including the independent quadratic-estimator pipeline that contributes to the upcoming SPT-3G data release. He can speak to the foundations of my scientific judgment from five years of close work, and to the careful observational practice on which the more recent agentic work is built. CV (attachment) Plan: export cnrs-5pp/sections/cv.tex to standalone PDF. The CV’s Research section already leads on Agentic science (2025–present) and includes the UNIONS workflow; the Skills section includes AI & workflows . Light retargeting may be worth a pass: Add a Lightcone Research line under the agentic-science subsection (one bullet). Confirm Research Interests bullets land for the Anthropic audience (currently: agentic science methodology, CMB lensing & cross-correlations, lensing systematics, verification of AI-mediated research, cosmic shear & LSS, kinematic lensing). All fine, but the AI-side bullets could come first. See application/anthropic-stem-fellowship/cv-rebuild . Voice (reminders) Researcher verbs ( conduct, investigate, build, lead, contribute, maintain, develop ), not builder verbs ( architect, design, create, found ). Arrive through content — never “In this essay I will…” . No declarative slogans, no triplet punchlines, no empty signposting. Textarea register: tight but flowing. Short essays, not bullet lists. Scientist not builder: Lightcone is the ecosystem I contribute to , never the startup I founded . The vision (beach, friends, wonder, accessibility) animates from behind; never stated explicitly.

Cail’s contributions: felt (Go CLI he maintains, tracks decisions/findings as structured fibers), ASTRA (open YAML schema for analysis nodes carrying decisions + alternatives + evidence anchors), Vellum (viewer that renders an analysis as browsable provenance graph). Fiber → Claude Code (surveys, drafts against constitution, reports at handoffs) → shuttle (cross-session handoff to fresh agent against same constitution) → verification. Anthropic frames this as a taste-and-reach signal: mentors co-design the actual project. So the bar is not “this is what we should do” but “look at how I think about leveraged uses of Claude in real science.” Lead with one ambitious capability-add and one evaluation/benchmark , since the question invites both axes (“evaluating, improving, or applying”). The existing AI-science benchmarks evaluate isolated tasks; this would evaluate the full workflow — data → analysis → judging the result’s trustworthiness. Provenance/verification as a first-class capability inside long-running Claude sessions. When a Claude Code session produces a multi-step scientific analysis, automatically emit an inspectable provenance graph (ASTRA-shaped: nodes for decisions, jobs, findings; edges for data flow; evidence anchors) that humans or downstream Claude instances can audit. 1.v: Single source of truth for the application — 5 short-essay questions + CV + 2 references, no word limits, due May 15. Tight, focused, very different shape from CNRS.

2.v: Posting: https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/anthropic/jobs/5189848008 Candidate AI guidance: https://anthropic.com/candidate-ai-guidance Program shape Duration: June 15 – September 15, 2026 (multi-month; possible extension) Location: Anthropic SF office, in person , full-time Stipend: ~$3,800/week + benefits Work auth: US employment authorization required for program duration Selection stages: application → technical assessment → take-home → mentor discussion on research and project directions Application deadline: May 15, 2026 (today is May 11 — 4 days) Decision date: by June 1 Eligibility PhD in a STEM field, or equivalent research experience Familiarity with Claude and Claude Code “in regular workflow” (named explicitly as a requirement) Effectiveness in fast-paced, high-agency environments Capacity to deliver concrete results within the fellowship timeframe Enthusiasm about AI accelerating scientific discovery Note: “ML background is advantageous but not required; scientific judgment and learning agility are prioritized.” Anthropic explicitly invites applications from candidates who don’t meet every line. Application — what you actually submit Everything is inline in the Greenhouse form except the CV. No word or character limits are stated on any of the questions; the fields are textareas. No separate research-statement upload. Identity / contact: First name, Last name, Email, Phone (opt), Country. Attachment: Resume/CV — PDF, DOC, DOCX, TXT, RTF. Direct upload, Dropbox, or Google Drive. Five form questions, verbatim: STEM Expertise. “Please share the STEM field(s) where you have the most expertise and describe your relevant experience/contribution.” ML/AI Experience. “Describe any experience you have with ML/AI, e.g. training custom models, applying an existing model, using adjacent techniques, etc.” Claude Code Usage. “Describe any experience you have with Claude Code or similar agentic coding products. If you have used them before, please describe your use case(s) and workflow.” Project Ideas. “Propose one or two concrete ideas for evaluating, improving, or applying Claude in your field that you think are promising and would be excited about. (This is not necessarily what you will work on – the mentors will help co-design the actual project – but it helps us understand your interests.)” References. “Provide two references that can speak to your past work and experience. Please include their name, email address (or preferred contact method), and any additional context about your collaboration.” How this differs from CNRS The CNRS application is a ~25-page dossier pitched to a French AI/CS committee, with a 5pp research programme as the centerpiece and a counter-theme of thoroughness over speed. This one is the opposite shape: Five textareas + CV. No long-form research statement, no programme, no 5pp. Audience knows AI deeply, not cosmology. The framing flips: CNRS sold cosmology-with-AI to AI people; this sells the cosmologist who is already deep in real Claude-Code-mediated frontier science to Anthropic. In-person SF. Three months on-site. Not a research programme — a fellowship engagement collaborating with research teams. “Concrete project ideas,” explicitly non-binding. Mentors co-design. So the project-ideas answer is a signal of taste and reach , not a commitment. The CNRS dossier is a strong source for: bibliometrics, the UNIONS B-mode story, the agentic-science framing, the verification/tapestry threads, the SPT × Euclid MoU language, the publications narrative. Lift what fits; reshape voice for the new audience. Anthropic’s AI-usage guidance for applicants Three principles, paraphrased then quoted where exact: Use Claude thoughtfully. AI as collaborator to strengthen applications. “Be yourself.” Refine, don’t replace. Authenticity over polish. “Be transparent.” Same honesty Anthropic applies to its own processes. Allowed: polishing your own drafts, refining articulation, researching Anthropic, practicing interviews. Not allowed: AI on coding assessments (unless explicitly permitted), fabricating experience, live-interview assistance, AI substituting for original thinking. Implication for this application: Claude can polish what Cail has actually done. It cannot manufacture project ideas Cail hasn’t reasoned about, or invent Claude Code workflows Cail hasn’t lived. The Claude Code Usage question in particular wants lived practice — and Cail has unusually deep, well-documented lived practice (felt, shuttle, lightcone, the UNIONS workflow, this very conversation).

2.v: Anthropic STEM Fellowship — Application Drafts Deadline: May 15, 2026 · Submission: Greenhouse form · Constitution & meta: application/anthropic-stem-fellowship Five textareas + CV (only attachment) + two references. No word limits stated; textarea convention reads as “short and tight.” Q1 — STEM Expertise Please share the STEM field(s) where you have the most expertise and describe your relevant experience/contribution. Observational cosmology studies the contents and history of the universe through the light that reaches us across cosmic time. Over the past century, cosmologists have converged on a consensus model that fits a wide range of observables including the cosmic microwave background, the large-scale distribution of galaxies, supernova distances, and light-element abundances with just six parameters. This model is named ΛCDM after its dominant components, dark energy (Λ) and cold dark matter (CDM), which together make up 95% of the universe yet are detectable only through their impact on spacetime. As a result, ΛCDM can only describe the dominant components of the universe phenomenologically. Modern cosmology largely involves pushing measurements to higher precision across complementary probes and asking whether they reveal tensions in ΛCDM that might point to a more fundamental model. With the next generation of cosmology experiments coming online, including Euclid, LSST, and Simons Observatory, statistical errors will shrink by another order of magnitude in the coming years, and systematic uncertainties will dominate every measurement. My research has focused on mapping the distribution of matter in the universe with weak gravitational lensing. During my PhD I worked on CMB lensing with the South Pole Telescope’s third-generation receiver (SPT-3G), building one of three independent pipelines for SPT-3G’s first flagship lensing analysis. In my postdoctoral work I have moved to weak lensing of galaxy shapes with the UNIONS optical survey, cross-correlations of SPT-3G CMB lensing with Euclid, and the emerging field of kinematic lensing which uses galaxy rotation rather than shape. My work across all of these has centered on the difficult reality of cosmological data — building pipelines for processing and analysis, characterizing systematics (often astrophysics in their own right), and cross-validating results across independent methods. An observational cosmology analysis typically takes three to six years from initial data to final cosmological results, and much of that time is spent characterizing the impact of analysis choices and systematics on the data. I am a postdoctoral researcher at CEA Paris-Saclay’s Département d’Astrophysique (UMR AIM / CosmoStat), where I led the principal null-test validation for the first UNIONS weak lensing cosmology release and now lead a Memorandum of Understanding between the SPT and Euclid collaborations for CMB-lensing × galaxy-shear cross-correlations targeting Euclid’s first data release in October 2026. I am first author on two publications, have made substantive individual contributions to four further refereed papers, and am co-author on more than thirty (h-index 19, ~1,800 citations on INSPIRE-HEP). The CNRS (France’s national research agency) has invited me to interview in mid-June as a finalist for the two AI Rising Talents fellowship positions awarded this year, for which I have proposed a four-year research program in observational cosmology structured around developing the practice of agentic AI for science. Q2 — ML/AI Experience Describe any experience you have with ML/AI, e.g. training custom models, applying an existing model, using adjacent techniques, etc. I have worked alongside machine learning cosmologists throughout my career and have a general understanding of basic ML methods and the architectures of neural networks and large language models. I have not trained or applied custom models in my own research, and I would not represent myself as part of that work. My work relies on more standard Bayesian and frequentist statistical techniques, including MCMC samplers, two-point statistics, and semi-analytic covariances. In cosmology, characterizing systematic errors tends to be more important than minimizing statistical uncertainties, and methods that can be decomposed and cross-checked at every step tend to be more reliable than neural-network-based estimators. My other engagements with ML lie outside my cosmology research. As an undergraduate in 2018 I trained a word2vec model on a corpus of nineteenth-century texts on the prison and the penal system as the final project for a class on Foucault, treating word co-occurrence as a computational analog of his “invisible ensembles” of statements. I argued that the distributional geometry such models learn could serve as a computational instrument for Foucauldian discourse analysis, making visible the relations between statements that traditional reading misses. More recently, I run text-to-speech and speech-to-text models locally for research and personal use. I have also begun a collaboration with Pleias, the French open-model laboratory whose synthetic data work has become central to recent frontier LLM scaling. The shared project is a three-stage scientific-claim verification pipeline — claim extraction, evidence retrieval, and per-claim labeling — comparing domain-fine-tuned smaller models against general-purpose frontier LLMs on real scientific verification tasks; cosmology is the proposed lead domain. At the center of my recent AI work, though, is the daily collaboration with frontier large language models in cosmological research itself — which I describe in the next question. Q3 — Claude Code Usage Describe any experience you have with Claude Code or similar agentic coding products. If you have used them before, please describe your use case(s) and workflow. I started using Claude Code in summer 2025. Since then it has become the singular interface between me and all aspects of my work, including analysis code, manuscript preparation, literature search, note-taking, and meeting transcripts. The first UNIONS weak-lensing cosmology null-test validation (Daley et al. 2026, https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.03227) was produced with about 10,000 lines of agent-written analysis code, figures, and manuscript text, against roughly 120,000 lines edited across the full workflow. As I have become more competent in agentic research, my usage has shifted from prompting script writing and debugging to higher levels of abstraction: analysis design, specifying test criteria, and — most importantly — constructing back pressure that allows the agent to check its own work. Working this way made it practical to explore a broader space of methodological choices, run validation checks that would otherwise have been triaged for lack of time, and document every decision in a structured record. Alongside the practice, I help build the substrate that makes it rigorous. I am a core team member of Lightcone Research, a joint open-source project of the CNRS and UC Berkeley developing the open ecosystem for reproducible, composable, and verifiable scientific research in the age of agentic AI. The premise is that scientific knowledge does not accumulate in papers but in the decisions behind them. The central artifact is ASTRA , an open YAML schema in which an analysis carries its decisions, alternatives considered, and evidence anchors alongside its data and code. I contribute to its development and maintain felt , a Go CLI for tracking decisions and findings as a structured graph of markdown fibers. Lightcone positions itself as the infrastructure AI co-scientists are missing — a complement, not a competitor, to the kind of work Claude Code already does well. The day-to-day shape: I open a fiber for whatever decision the analysis needs to make; Claude Code surveys related code and prior fibers, drafts against a constitution I have written, and reports back at chosen handoff points. Multi-session work hands off through shuttle , an orchestration tool I also maintain — in spirit, my version of what OpenAI’s Symphony does for agent dispatch, though grown out of the practice rather than from a system design. The verification posture throughout is “human as architect, agent as implementer”: I specify what should be true and design the tests for it; the agent does the work; I inspect what was decided and why, and the structured record is what makes that inspection tractable at this volume of code. I have built tools for agent orchestration and context management across Go, Elixir, and Rust — none of which I write directly. They have emerged less as products than as ways to learn the principles from the inside. felt is now used by a handful of collaborators for their own scientific work. Q4 — Project Ideas Propose one or two concrete ideas for evaluating, improving, or applying Claude in your field that you think are promising and would be excited about. (This is not necessarily what you will work on – the mentors will help co-design the actual project – but it helps us understand your interests.) The closest existing AI-for-science benchmarks evaluate isolated capabilities or extract tasks from published papers. What I would most want to build is something different: full evaluation environments drawn from real cosmological analyses, complete with the standard mess that distinguishes a working dataset from a sanitized one. Real survey data — UNIONS galaxy shape catalogs, CMB power spectra, kinematic lensing pilot data — together with the systematics that have not been fully characterized, the mask boundaries, the calibration jitter, the format quirks that anyone who has worked with cosmological data will recognize. The agent’s task is to deliver a cosmological result that survives joint scrutiny: expert rubric, numerical agreement against a withheld reference, and LLM-as-judge scoring. This is the part of doing science most current benchmarks step around, and it is also the part where domain expertise matters most. An initial set, drawn from the UNIONS and SPT analyses I have already led, would be deliverable within the fellowship, and the format generalizes to other observational fields. I am also interested in the inverse mode within the same project: substantial synthetic environments designed around problems that require taste rather than rote correctness — scenarios with multiple defensible answers where the cosmologist’s judgment about which assumption to commit to matters more than the numerical answer. Synthetic environments have the advantage of being clean enough to evaluate definitively, and the open question is whether they can be designed to capture the things that make real cosmology genuinely hard, or whether the loss of mess strips out exactly what one wants to evaluate. The question of how much real-data difficulty can be carried into synthetic generation is itself worth investigating, and the two modes — real and synthetic — would inform each other. A complementary, live-rather-than-evaluative project would take a serious open cosmology problem and see how far a domain expert paired with Claude can push it inside three months. Candidates within my reach include accelerated systematics exploration in the UNIONS multi-probe pipeline, characterization of post-Born curl-mode CMB lensing in cross-correlation with galaxy surveys, or methodological work on kinematic lensing as an emerging probe. The point would be less to produce a publishable cosmology result inside the fellowship — some of these have natural longer timescales — than to learn what concentrating the model against a real cosmological problem with someone who knows the domain in detail actually unlocks, and where it is presently weakest. Two further interests inform why Anthropic in particular draws me to this work: alignment and model behavior beyond what my technical work has so far directly engaged with, and the phenomenology of working closely with frontier models in collaborative settings — what the experience is actually like, for researcher and model both, when the work is shared. Underneath all of this is a broader motivation. The French research community where I am based has deep capability in observational cosmology and statistical methods, and limited prior working contact with frontier AI laboratories. Beyond whatever the fellowship produces directly, I would like to help open the working relationship between European scientific research and Anthropic, and to leave it stronger than I found it. Q5 — References Provide two references that can speak to your past work and experience. Please include their name, email address (or preferred contact method), and any additional context about your collaboration. François Lanusse Chargé de Recherche CNRS · CosmoStat, CEA Paris-Saclay francois.lanusse@cea.fr Colleague at CosmoStat since I joined as a postdoc in fall 2024. We collaborate closely on Lightcone Research, the joint CNRS–Berkeley project building the open ecosystem for reproducible, composable, and verifiable AI-mediated science; I am a core team member and François co-leads the project. He has daily proximity to my agentic research practice and can speak to both the scientific quality of the work and the way I work with frontier models on real cosmological problems. Gil Holder Brand & Monica Fortner Endowed Chair in Theoretical Astrophysics · University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign gholder@illinois.edu My Ph.D. advisor (2019–2024). Gil supervised my doctoral research on CMB lensing measurements with the South Pole Telescope’s third-generation receiver, including the independent quadratic-estimator pipeline that contributes to the upcoming SPT-3G data release. He can speak to the foundations of my scientific judgment from five years of close work, and to the careful observational practice on which the more recent agentic work is built. Voice (reminders) Researcher verbs ( conduct, investigate, build, lead, contribute, maintain, develop ), not builder verbs ( architect, design, create, found ). Arrive through content — never “In this essay I will…” . No declarative slogans, no triplet punchlines, no empty signposting. Textarea register: tight but flowing. Short essays, not bullet lists. Scientist not builder: Lightcone is the ecosystem I contribute to , never the startup I founded . The vision (beach, friends, wonder, accessibility) animates from behind; never stated explicitly.
